How to Increase Your Google AdSense Revenue (Placement Tips)
A short notes post on the small things that move the needle on blog AdSense revenue — mostly about ad placement.
Google AdSense is the go-to for anyone running a blog. It pays members via impressions and clicks. I covered initial setup in
How to Add Google AdSense to Hexo NexT (article in Mandarin)
AdSense covers a lot of surfaces — Blogspot (Google's own blog platform), YouTube — you'll see AdSense's footprint everywhere.
because they auto-detect your layout and can serve a wider variety of ad sizes, which typically beats the other three on revenue.
Display ads also support fixed sizes, but I'd recommend
— let AdSense analyze the page and pick. Google itself notes that "fixed-size ad units may serve fewer ad varieties, which could lead to lower earnings than responsive ad units".
So no matter what — responsive ads over fixed-size ads.
Now you've picked the type. Placement matters. Key principles:
Pick spots that get reliably seen.
Don't ask readers to click your ads.
This one trips people up — more ads should mean more revenue, right?

Sort of. More ads do bring in more revenue — up to a point. Past that, ads overwhelm readers. They get lost in sound and motion: "Where am I? Who am I? Where's the article?" I've landed on blog home pages that were just a wall of ads. I literally couldn't figure out how to click into anything and just left. Your blog stops mattering at that point, unless your content is genuinely irreplaceable.
When someone's reading an article, they're focused. Drop an ad mid-content and you break their flow — and many readers will just bail.
Worse, they'll add your site to their AdBlock list — and AdBlocked impressions don't pay either.
Think about where readers' eyes always land. For my site, that's the
top and bottom of the article

Top of article generally gets more views than bottom. Most readers hit my site via Google search. Worth checking your own breakdown in Google Analytics. For me — most of my traffic is keyword-driven, so the article-top placement performs best, with bottom close behind.
Performance-wise, the sidebar is actually the best — it's visible everywhere (home page, tags, categories, articles), while in-article ads only show when a reader clicks into an article.
In the chart, article-top still beats sidebar, even though sidebar shows on every page. Why? Because the average screen size doesn't fully reveal the sidebar position:

The ad's technically there, but partial visibility doesn't count as an impression — so it doesn't earn. Article top, on the other hand, is always seen when someone reads an article. Sidebar only counts when the user scrolls and the ad fully enters view.

I've seen plenty of blogs use popups to force ad views. Effective? Maybe. Annoying? Definitely. I was hunting for some info recently and the first thing the site did was throw a popup ad in my face. I noped out.
No "click my ads, please" and no clicking your own
These are the two AdSense rules you really shouldn't break. If you do —
from AdSense. Re-applying typically doesn't work either. (Every AdSense user has to be approved.)

Google doesn't publish their full review criteria — public criteria invite gaming. So nobody knows the exact line, but these two — don't ask for clicks, don't click your own — are the clearest tripwires.

A short notes post on the small things that move the needle on blog AdSense revenue — mostly about ad placement. Covers picking the right ad type (responsive Display ads), how many ads is too many, slots that are reliably visible (sidebar, article top, article bottom), and the two Google AdSense rules you should never break (no asking users to click, no clicking your own ads).
